preamble: The transformation from a voiced alveolar fricative to an alveolar flap is a classic example of rhotacism, a process where sibilants or other consonants shift into rhotic sounds, most famously documented in Latin and Germanic languages.

examples.note: Intervocalic /s/ voiced to /z/ before shifting completely to the rhotic /r/.
The Proto-Germanic /z/ (often from /s/ via Verner's Law) shifted to /r/ in the North and West Germanic branches.

sources: Weiss, M. (2009). Outline of the Historical and Comparative Grammar of Latin.
Ringe, D. (2006). From Proto-Indo-European to Proto-Germanic.
